data.title: Here Group: Early In The IP Curve, With Enlight JV As A Wildcard
data.description:
I love my job as an analyst because it constantly pushes me into places I'd never willingly go on my own. Like a bearded guy scrolling through Here Group (HERE) website, analyzing Wakuku’s new collection.

This is my life now. And I just hope no one checks my browser history.

But at least I can finally understand what drove the post-earnings drop this time. And, if you ask me, I think the market is quite impatient with this story here.

Besides the recent sale of its 'Silver Economy' assets at dirt-cheap prices, Here Group is opening more physical stores than the market would like.

And no, this isn't inherently bad.

It seems like a huge acceleration from the 3 stores that opened in the last quarter and perhaps a sign that the plan for ~4 permanent stores might be stretched a bit here and there, in addition to the pop-ups.

It was a slip of ~1,000 bp. A slump for a business that would operate at a mature gross margin of over 50%.

Pop Mart (PMRTY) operates at nearly 70% gross margin, and, as I told you in a previous article, Here Group would probably never reach that level.

Three very simple things are still holding them back: scale, less-known IPs, and pricing.

And it is on these first two targets that Here Group is focusing on the early stage of Wakuku. This is the second quarter as pure-pop toys.

But besides the margin slip, I think the guidance for Q3 FY 2026 was also a bit alarming.

The CFO guided it at RMB 140 to 150 million. It doesn't seem too bad at first glance. Converting it, it's somewhere between $20.3 and $21.7 million. But for a business that was growing at double-digit rates quarter-over-quarter (almost 40% in the last reported earnings), this drop of at least 15% is something that makes us scratch our heads.

An analyst even noticed this in the earnings call. The CEO said it's a seasonal fluctuation during the Spring Festival.

I'm not very familiar with Chinese retail, but I imagine there's really some slowdown in Q3 FY 2026 between January 29th and February 12th. And, with some anticipation of inventory in Q2, it may have even inflated the results a bit.

But I see something else here too.

Here doesn't have any blowup IP releases for the next quarter like they had before.

'WAKUKU On A Roll,' which they released at the end of November, likely peaked in December (including stores stocking up for the New Year) and then slowed down at the beginning of the year.

The Market is Impatient, but IP Takes Time

Here Group is following a flywheel-style IP model. You can see it in how often they talk about storytelling and the openings of their pop-up stores.

I feel that's what Labubu (Wakuku's 'rich cousin') lacked.

Even with a movie announced, Labubu became famous too early, and I think they couldn't develop a story.

Now they have a movie announced, and Pop Mart's biggest concern should be whether there will still be interest when the movie is released. And if the movie is released hastily, something bad could dilute the IP.

Wakuku is different. They aren't a globally known IP, but they seem to be something that's becoming mainstream within the niche (especially in some Asian countries).

So, I think they still have time to build Wakuku and other IPs without rushing and hurting the brand. If something flops but still grabs attention, who really cares?

That’s what matters at the end of the day.

Being backed by a lot of cash helps, but I have a hunch that they're waiting for some catalyst.

Here Group is already testing some collaborations with a Chinese jewelry company called Lukfook (with Wakuku, of course), but nothing is more important than the joint venture with Enlight Media.

If you don't know them, Enlight produced Nezha 2 (the highest-grossing movie of last year).

Even if they don't make a movie, perhaps an animated series could fill storytelling and awareness gaps. It might also open doors for other IPs in the future; who knows?

For me, it's something that has a better chance of succeeding than a Labubu movie.

The audience is more niche (probably families in China and Southeast Asia), and expectations are low or nonexistent.

(...) The joint venture within Enlight Media, you mentioned it's exactly one of the specific projects to carry out our product and content stewardship strategy. We hope to explore more possibilities for our IPs in areas like the film and the television contact and derivative development through such cooperation. As for specific future plans, we will disclose them to the market when there is a substantial progress.

I think Enlight could even start with some kind of low-risk Douyin streaming.

Even more so when Wakuku still accounts for ~73% of the company's revenue. If the Wakuku audience becomes saturated, they will have a big problem ahead.

Ideally, they would release some content in ~1 to 2 years at most. A joint venture is a great sign, but with nothing on the table, it's good to continue monitoring and not build castles in the sand.

Some Projections for FY 2026

To begin, it's clear that despite being very bullish (a consensus of 'Strong Buy' among analysts), it seems Wall Street has finally become a bit more conservative on Here Group's sales.

While management guided it at approximately $108.7 million (RMB 750 million) for the entire FY 2026, analysts are now targeting something closer to $109.1 million. Much more conservative than the $335 million in November.

If they pulled $17.9 million in Q1 and $25.3 million in Q2, they still have $65.5 million left for H2 FY 2026. Doing the math here and considering they actually reached RMB 140-150 million in Q3 ($21 million midpoint), it seems that a revenue of $44.5 million in Q4 is implied.

Do they have any launches in mind? Do they have any blockbuster products for Q4? We still don't know anything.

But it seems a bit stretched to expect them to reach that in Q4, and that's precisely why my biggest fear is a downward revision here.

And look, this has nothing to do with the long-term story. I just don't know if they're anchored to the last few quarters.

So, I think I'll assume that perhaps a bear scenario would be something close to $90 million for the whole of FY 2026. That will give me some margin of safety.

As I imagine the pop-up store model won't be pulled back, perhaps Here will have gross margins of ~30 to 35% in FY 2026 (gross profit of $32.4 to $37.8 million based on guided revenue and $27 to $31.5 million in the bear scenario).

SG&A will probably be diluted to almost 40% of sales. And, right at the bottom line, R&D will also be at 5%. (That alone accounts for $48.6 million eating up all of their gross profit).

They probably won't break even in FY 2026. I believe that should happen in FY 2027 (slightly higher gross margins and SG&A being diluted if sales continue to increase).

For example, if the Wall Street consensus is indeed correct about revenue of $240 million in FY 2027, and they achieve a ~35-40% gross margin (gross profit of $84-$96 million) and SG&A + R&D is ~20-30% of sales, EBIT would most likely finally be above $20 million. Since they have no significant debt, this would translate into profit and, more importantly, cash.

So, everything will depend on two things:

  • Will sales continue to grow? (launches pipeline, critical success of Wakuku, and well-executed distribution)
  • Will the brick-and-mortar store model continue to represent a large portion of sales?

I understand what the bears are seeing here. The brick-and-mortar model makes them operationally leveraged. Just one drawback is sales and costs rise.

This risk is greatly mitigated if partnerships like MINISO's (MNSO) had a larger chunk of sales.

Cash Burn: How Much Could They Take?

Here it is not yet profitable.

This is a fact, but also expected for companies that have recently pivoted. My prediction is that they will reach break-even in FY 2027.

I think it's interesting to take a look at the cash burn.

And it even seems like another pivot I've been following. LuxExperience (LUXE) also hasn't reached break-even and, like Here, is also sitting on a lot of cash.

But here we have some additional limitations. I have no idea about the EBITDA-to-OCF conversion as a pure-pop toy (Here Group publishes the cash flow statement only at the end of each fiscal year).

So, I need to look only at the cash burn and not model based on FCF.

In Q1 FY 2026, for example, Here Group had $110.7 million in cash.

The drop was from $6.6 million quarter-over-quarter to $104.1 million.

But remember, it seems Here is at the bottom of its margin range with a higher mix of pop-up stores. This might increase in Q3 FY 2026, but I think it should decrease slightly in Q4 as they reach their guidance target.

If I project this cash burn going forward (something quite unlikely, since they could simply reduce SG&A), Here Group could withstand almost 16 quarters without relying on any debt.

Break-even is likely in FY 2027; I imagine they could burn just under $20 million going forward.

Some dilution might come from SBC, but it doesn't seem to be anything to worry about since the share count has remained stable for at least two years. Here probably compensate for this by buying back some stock here and there after the post-conversion from Class A stock to Class B (the latter being publicly traded).

Cheaper Than Ever?

As a narrative-driven story (all that talk about IP incubation and potential licensing I mentioned before), it's difficult to say how much the stock is worth. In these cases, if you think it's worth the bet (very small weight in your portfolio, mind you!) based on the investment case, it's still a good 'Buy.'

But you know I'm a bit stubborn. I'll try to give some quantitative insights here and there.

The first thing to consider is that the stock is trading at much lower multiples than before.

For example, if you really follow management's guidance of $108 million and nail the $99 million EV they're trading at, the EV/S multiple here is ~0.9x.

That's really quite low for companies of this type.

The market probably isn't pricing in Wakuku consolidating in China/Southeast Asia or the joint venture with Enlight.

If I compare it with other toymakers like Bloks (listed only in HK and producing toys based on its own and third-party IPs), they trade at 3.9x sales. Giants like BANDAI NAMCO (NCBDF) and Mattel (MAT) trade at slightly lower multiples (between 1 and 2x sales).

I think we need to separate what really matters here for now, and that's Wakuku. SIINONO is growing very fast, but it's still a very small chunk of Here Group's sales.

If Wakuku had brand equity in content + licensing today, I would imagine explosive multiples. Maybe ~3 to 4x sales or more if the movie/series had already been released and a more engaged community.

We're still early, so I'm not counting my chickens before they hatch.

Wakuku's sales are growing ~44% compared to the last quarter. If I annualize that (being very conservative here), I'd arrive at revenue of almost RMB 520 million ($75.4 million).

Now, I think at least 2x sales is fair if this strong growth continues, not considering an explosion in popularity.

That's at least $150.8 million for Wakuku alone.

Today, Here Group is trading at less than that. It seems that Wakuku itself is being priced as a fad within the whole basket. And I'd even concede lower multiples for other IPs, but like it or not, Wakuku is selling well.

The market is punishing not the growth, but the quality of their growth.

If the pop-up stores are truly temporary and the 'original' gross margin of their business model is ~34 to 40%, we don't have much to worry about in the short term.

Optionality is asymmetrical for me, and at this price I maintain my 'Buy' rating.

If I were to predict something close to 1.5x sales for Here (not for Wakuku), already considering some haircut for the low cap and float, the price target would be around $4.80 in the short term.

It's a smaller target than Wall Street aims for.

And don't think I've forgotten about my bear case.

If revenue reaches $90 million, a multiple range of 1 to 1.5x sales (priced as low-growth or mature toymakers) would give me a price target between $3.50 and $4.30.

I think it could go much higher if Wakuku takes off.

As 'cute monster' IP movies become fashionable again, the joint venture with Enlight is gold. Labubu might maintain its popularity in the West, but in Mainland China they could push Wakuku, and given Nezha's success there, it seems like a world apart.

Risks

As you can see, I don't think either the re-rating potential of pure-pop toys or the licensor's potential is priced in here.

But like any small-cap stock (especially in a potential bear market), there are some real risks on the table.

And, as I always say in my coverage at Here Group, this isn't a bet to concentrate your portfolio on. Just ~1 or 2% is enough.

Just to mention a few for you:

  • The concentration of sales on Wakuku is a big deal. The biggest risk is saturating the audience before they've built the narrative universe. If Wakuku fails, the story falls apart (at least in the short/medium term).
  • Pop-up store model cannot be seen as the biggest driver of sales. I understand they need to increase awareness, but this also structurally compresses the gross margin. Wall Street doesn't want that. Even if it doesn't grow as much, perhaps improving the quality of earnings would help the stock more.
  • Responsibility for accurate guidance has been pushed to Q4 since the next quarter is expected to slow down.
  • It's an ADR with low float. So, expect it to remain volatile in the short term.

Most of the upside should come in the medium/long term after they work on the Wakuku IP with Enlight. Here needs some time, and it seems the market will reward them once they reach profitability.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sea Limited: Profitability And Positive FCF Growing Strongly
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Virtus SGA Global Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Sea Limited (SE), a Southeast Asian consumer internet company with an integrated ecosystem combining e-commerce, digital payments, and entertainment. Sea has a diversified business model, with its Shopee e-commerce platform, a mobile-centric marketplace that provides integrated payments, logistics infrastructure, and seller services; Garena, a digital entertainment platform for users to access mobile and PC online games; and Monee digital financial services offering credit, mobile wallets, payment processing, banking, and insurtech services. Sea operates in markets with unique geopolitical, cultural, and economic complexities, and the company is ahead in localizing its products and content, making it difficult for competitors to disrupt at scale. Sea's e-commerce business benefits from low penetration rates and rising digital adoption, while its logistics network and localized approach create meaningful competitive advantages that are difficult for competitors to replicate. The company's digital financial services segment is scaling rapidly, leveraging accumulated user data and e-commerce use cases to drive engagement and profitability. In gaming, Sea's deep understanding of local audiences and self-developed franchises like Free Fire are favorable for emerging markets audiences where low-end smartphones dominate. While near-term margin volatility is expected as Sea invests in logistics, fulfillment, and new initiatives such as free shipping membership, management's focus on building long-term competitive advantages and expanding addressable markets positions the company well for sustainable growth.

Among the key risk considerations are increasing competition from TikTok and Lazada (backed by Alibaba (BABA) (BABA)) in Southeast Asia, MercadoLibre in Brazil, and Coupang (CPNG) in Taiwan. TikTok has been able to rapidly grow market share leveraging its user traffic on social media and the market share dynamic between Shopee, TikTok, and potential new entrants needs to be closely considered. We are also monitoring the unique political, macroeconomic, and currency risks across the multiple markets Sea operates in. Additionally, a potential slowdown in the U.S. and China could weaken the region's economic development and lead to depreciation in local currencies. We believe the company is positioned to benefit from low market penetration and an above average GDP growth rate across its geographies.

Profitability and positive free cash flow are growing strongly supported by the company's earlier investments, which we expect will continue to drive repeatable revenues from a large installed base. We plan to build the position opportunistically moving forward.

data.title: Broadcom: Focus On Organic Growth And AI Position It Well
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Virtus SGA Global Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

During the quarter, we initiated a below-average weight position in leading semiconductor and infrastructure software company Broadcom (AVGO). Broadcom's ongoing transformation from a provider of custom ASICs to a full rack-scale solutions vendor, including scale-up networking, marks a significant improvement in its strategic positioning compared to earlier in the year. This strategic evolution has been validated by recent product launches, such as the Scale-up Ethernet solution, and committed orders from major customers like Anthropic.

Broadcom is also diversifying its customer base, expanding from three to five qualified customers with purchase commitments, and accelerating production volume timelines for future prospective clients. The shift in AI demand from training to inferencing is creating more visible and stable revenue streams, as inferencing workloads have grown rapidly, driven by innovations in long context windows and agentic workloads.

Broadcom's business model is supported by strong pricing power, recurring revenues, and robust growth opportunities. The company maintains industry-leading margins, with semiconductor gross margin around 70% and software gross margin near 90%, leveraging high technology barriers and strong customer relationships.

Recurring revenue is underpinned by long-term AI semiconductor engagements and high-retention infrastructure software subscriptions. As AI spending shifts toward inference, revenue visibility and stability improve, backed by real use cases and multi-year customer commitments. Broadcom's growth is driven by rising AI demand, as major labs and hyperscalers adopt custom ASICs for both training and inference. The company's ability to integrate a full systems solution from ASICs to networking provides an advantage over other vendors, and its strategy of building sustainable franchises ensures resilience against market and technology shifts.

While competition and customer concentration remain risks, Broadcom is actively expanding its custom ASIC engagements to improve diversification. Broadcom's strategic evolution and focus on organic growth and AI position it well for long-term stability and profitability, supporting over 20% revenue and earnings growth.

data.title: Meta's Agentic AI Ambitions
data.description:
When Meta (META) started building their Superintelligence team (MSL) in mid-2025, I certainly expected to see faster progress than what we have seen from them so far. I wonder if the Llama 4 fiasco, as well as the backlash for “Vibes” last year, made Meta much more tentative than usual for releasing new products/features. This is making Meta increasingly look like a laggard in the AI race despite spending commensurate capital on compute and talent compared to other players leading the race.

Despite looking like a laggard, Meta also seems to be on a tuck-in acquisition spree every other week these days. Just in March, Meta acquired Moltbook, a social media platform for AI agents, and made a non-exclusive license deal with Dreamer, the startup that helps consumers build AI agents.

Since the Dreamer co-founders joined MSL as part of the deal, this is essentially yet another roundabout “acquisition” Silicon Valley invented in the current antitrust environment. The fact that Dreamer raised $56 million at a $500 million valuation back in December 2024 and Dreamer’s CEO was a former CTO of Stripe makes me think Meta may have paid a good premium to get the Dreamer team in MSL.

Just last week, Dreamer’s now former CEO demoed the product in this podcast, and it indeed looks quite promising. In fact, while reacting to the acquisition news, Alex Heath mentioned the following in his newsletter:

I was recently given a demo of the product by co-founder (and former Stripe CTO) David Singleton. I was so intrigued that I demanded to be added to the beta. I clearly wasn’t the only one who was impressed.

With the Manus and Dreamer deals, it appears Meta is getting more serious about agent infrastructure above the model layer. In fact, even if Meta remains a laggard in building a SOTA model for the foreseeable future, their core business continues to offer compelling opportunities to integrate AI on top of the model layer.

Even though Meta may seem largely absent in the current agentic AI fever, a blog post (h/t Eric Seufert) from Meta last week made me appreciate how it is already using AI agents in their advertising infrastructure. Some key excerpts from Meta’s blog post (emphasis mine):

Optimizing these ML models has traditionally been time-consuming. Engineers craft hypotheses, design experiments, launch training runs, debug failures across complex codebases, analyze results and iterate. Each full cycle can span days to weeks. As Meta’s models have matured over the years, finding meaningful improvements has become increasingly challenging. The manual, sequential nature of traditional ML experimentation has become a bottleneck to innovation.To address this, Meta built the Ranking Engineer Agent (REA), an autonomous AI agent designed to drive the end-to-end ML lifecycle and iteratively evolve Meta’s ads ranking models at scale.REA addresses three core challenges in autonomous ML experimentation:Long-Horizon, Asynchronous Workflow Autonomy: ML training jobs run for hours or days, far beyond what any session-bound assistant can manage. REA maintains persistent state and memory across multiround workflows spanning days or weeks, staying coordinated without continuous human supervision.High-Quality, Diverse Hypothesis Generation: Experiment quality is only as good as the hypothesis that drives it. REA synthesizes outcomes from historical experiments and frontier ML research to surface configurations unlikely to emerge from any single approach, and it improves with every iteration.Resilient Operation Within Real-World Constraints: Infrastructure failures, unexpected errors and compute budgets can’t halt an autonomous agent. REA adapts within predefined guardrails, keeping workflows moving without escalating routine failures to humans.In the first production validation across a set of six models, REA-driven iterations doubled average model accuracy over baseline approaches. This translates directly to stronger advertiser outcomes and better experiences on Meta platforms.REA amplifies impact by automating the mechanics of ML experimentation, enabling engineers to focus on creative problem-solving and strategic thinking. Complex architectural improvements that previously required multiple engineers over several weeks can now be completed by smaller teams in days.Early adopters using REA increased their model-improvement proposals from one to five in the same time frame. Work that once took two engineers per model now takes three engineers across eight models.

One of the interesting bits from the blog post is that for long-horizon workflow autonomy, Meta built REA on an internal AI agent framework called “Confucius,” which they elaborated further on in this paper back in February 2026.

Often, when tech companies try to improve AI coders, they focus on making the underlying AI models (like GPT or Claude) smarter. However, the paper argued that the “scaffolding” i.e., the software environment, memory systems, and tools built around the AI, is just as important.

When working on big codebases, AI agents frequently get overwhelmed by reading too much code, forget their original plan during long tasks, or repeat the same mistakes.

The most interesting takeaway from the paper is that a great setup can compensate for a less powerful AI. The researchers proved that a weaker model (Claude 4.5 Sonnet) using the Confucius scaffolding successfully fixed more bugs (52.7%) than a stronger, more expensive model (Claude 4.5 Opus) using Anthropic’s standard setup (52.0%).

When powered by the GPT-5.2 model, Confucius Code Agent successfully resolved 59% of the real-world bugs on the SWE-Bench-Pro test, beating both prior academic research and the official corporate systems built by OpenAI and Anthropic under identical conditions.

If such scaffolding itself can consistently beat the more expensive SOTA models, it can provide a ceiling on SOTA model developers’ ability to exercise pricing power. It remains to be seen whether such scaffolding can outperform more expensive SOTA models in a wide range of scenarios.

Nonetheless, the key takeaway is quite encouraging for all the tech companies that will not have a SOTA model, and those tech companies may still be able to capture value from better scaffolding.

Back in 2Q’25 call, Zuckerberg mentioned the following:

Over the last few months, we’ve begun to see glimpses of our AI systems improving themselves. And the improvement is slow for now, but undeniable and developing superintelligence

The paper and the blog post essentially validated Zuckerberg’s claims made a couple of quarters ago. The autonomous and self-recursive nature of REA is yet another indication that Meta has a pretty good shot at monetizing their capex even if they remain a laggard in building SOTA models.

Perhaps to hint at their confidence, Meta yesterday disclosed a new stock options program for their Chief Technology Officer Andrew Bosworth, Chief Product Officer Chris Cox, Chief Operation Officer Javier Olivan, Chief Financial Officer Susan Li, Chief Legal Officer C.J. Mahoney and Vice Chairman Dina Powell McCormick.

While Meta historically largely doled out RSUs, this is a welcome change from shareholders perspective, as the options would expire worthless if the stock price doesn’t exceed the hurdles.

To receive even the first tranche of the options, Meta stock needs to increase by 86% from the current price, and to receive the full options package, the stock needs to be 6x the current price within the next five years! For your reference, I am showing the stock options program for the CTO below (although the numbers vary for each executive, the structure is quite similar for other executives)

As a minority shareholder of Meta, I also very much appreciate that Zuckerberg excluded himself from this options program, as he probably doesn’t need any more incentive to see Meta thrive.

data.title: Virtus SGA Global Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

The fourth quarter of 2025 highlighted extreme market divergence, with cyclical and momentum-driven assets dominating while quality growth strategies faced continued headwinds from sales stability underperformance, ending 2025 as the most challenging year for the portfolio since its inception in 2011.

Highlights

  • Returns for Q4 were -0.1% (Gross) and -0.9% (Net) compared to the MSCI ACWI return of 3.3% and the MSCI ACWI Growth return of 2.8%.
  • Since inception, annualized returns stand at 11.5% (Gross) and 8.2% (Net), compared to 9.8% (MSCI ACWI) and 11.8% for the MSCI ACWI Growth.
  • Largest contributors for the quarter were Alphabet (GOOGL), TSMC (TSM), and Fast Retailing (FRCOY).
  • Largest detractors for the quarter were Arm Holdings (ARM), Microsoft (MSFT), and MercadoLibre (MELI).
  • Initiations – New holdings were introduced in Broadcom (AVGO), Sea Limited (SE), and Netflix (NFLX).
  • Liquidations – ServiceNow (NOW) and Infosys (INFY) were liquidated in the quarter.
  • The Global Growth portfolio is forecasted to achieve 12% revenue growth and 16% earnings growth annually over the next three years consistent with historical results.
  • SGA's portfolio of reliable compounders is trading at its steepest ever discount relative to the MSCI ACWI P/E, while many cyclicals are trading at peak multiples just as CapEx growth is poised to slow. We believe this dynamic creates a favorable risk-reward opportunity in the portfolio today.

Market Environment: Extremes Beneath the Surface

The fourth quarter of 2025 marked the end of a particularly challenging year for quality growth investment strategies. This period was defined by an extraordinary divergence in market returns. Bond proxies and defensive compounders performed poorly, while non-profitable technology, meme stocks, commodity-linked equities, and high-beta cyclicals delivered extraordinary returns. This divergence highlights a market driven less by fundamentals and more by liquidity, positioning, and fund flows.

The global economic landscape in 2025 defied earlier pessimistic forecasts. Growth expectations gradually increased, supported by resilient consumer spending and easing monetary policies implemented by the Federal Reserve, the Bank of England, and the European Central Bank. With all three major central banks in easing mode, global liquidity improved materially. Monetary support, combined with ongoing fiscal stimulus in the United States and new support measures in China, created powerful tailwinds for cyclical assets.

This environment favored cyclical industries indiscriminately. Banks, industrials, materials, and lower-quality balance sheet exposures rallied sharply on expectations of a soft economic landing, while high-quality companies with stable earnings growth received less attention. Market leadership broadened within cyclicals, even as leadership narrowed at the index level.

Cyclical Dominance in 2025

Despite this optimism, the narrative of uninterrupted acceleration carries risks. Inflation has not been fully subdued, and aggressive fiscal and monetary policies could reignite pricing pressures. Trade tensions and tariff uncertainty continue to threaten global supply chains. Current market pricing reflects an optimistic scenario with limited margin for error, particularly when contrasted with the valuation compression affecting high-quality growth holdings.

This is another way of observing the size and momentum effects of a few large winners affecting market capitalization weighted indices in a 'winner takes all' way irrespective of rising valuations of the successful few companies leading the market's progress. Many active managers underperform in these conditions despite sticking to successful, tried and tested processes. This creates a coiled spring effect where in the fullness of time the market reverts to a 'weighing machine' rather than a 'voting machine' as Benjamin Graham famously explained. Patience is a virtue under such circumstances hence the observation that the stock market is a device for transferring money from the impatient to the patient. As cyclical multiples reach new peaks just as AI capital expenditure growth slows just, we expect to see a reversal in the trends that have been driving markets over the last few years and hurting our relative performance. For this reason, we remain patient and are increasingly optimistic about our prospects in 2026 and beyond.

Extreme concentration and momentum not only create headline winners, but also a long tail of significant losers, often independent of company fundamentals and driven instead by passive flows, factor rotations, and crowding effects. While cyclicals and momentum continued to run, quality factors such as sales stability, a proxy for repeatable revenues, and high gross margins, a proxy for pricing power, continued to lag, proving a stiff headwind for portfolio returns.

AI Capital Expenditures Update

Our view is that the current trend of AI-related CapEx growth is not sustainable and will moderate moving forward. There are three structural constraints we expect will limit growth in spending moving forward: power availability, skilled labor shortages, and capital availability. These constraints are central to SGA's view that AI CapEx growth rates will decelerate over time, naturally slowing cyclical momentum rather than collapsing demand outright.

A good portion of the current CapEx spending by the hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet, Meta (META)) is directed toward securing power generation, grid access, and data center real estate – assets with multi-decade useful lives. These are long-term investments meant to ensure scarce and strategically important resources that are deemed necessary for future competition. Recent transactions, such as Alphabet's acquisition of Intersect, underscore the growing importance of power access. While such deals help at the margin, they are unlikely to fully resolve power bottlenecks, which we expect to persist for years. The shortages of skilled tradesmen such as electricians, welders, and plumbers in the construction industry, estimated to be around 400,000+ by The Associated Builders and Contractors trade group, is also pushing out project timelines and will make it harder to keep up with current demand for power generation and data center construction. According to insights gleaned from a recent research trip to the country's preeminent utility conference, lead times for new power installation are extending from 2-3 years to 4-6 years due to labor shortages.

Lastly, with hyperscaler CapEx spending having reached historically high proportions of revenues and operating cash flow, the lack of incremental capital availability to be deployed towards CapEx is an important factor which we expect will constrain the rate of growth on future spending.

Capital Expended: U.S. Hyperscalers Forecast to Utilize 90% of Operating Cash Flows

While the CapEx is still filtering down various cyclical industries, further upward growth revisions are challenging. And, therefore, purely cyclical sectors whose growth rate is exposed to the growth rate of hyperscaler CapEx will have a shorter runway of growth left.

We acknowledge that these expenditures have shifted the hyperscalers into a more capital-intensive business model. However, as return on invested capital remains high along with 30-40% operating margins and strong cash flow generation across their businesses, we still view their future return potential as attractive. That said, we are actively monitoring key risks such as the select use of off-balance sheet financing, customer concentration and quality, technological obsolescence given rapid advancements in chip technology, utilization rates, and depreciation schedules and their impact on earnings. We view risks across these dimensions and their possible impact to future growth expectations to be low currently.

Finally, our view remains that the most attractive long-term AI opportunities reside not with companies exposed to cyclical swings, but with businesses that are building long-term value. In 2025, portfolio holdings Nvidia (NVDA) and Alphabet benefited significantly from their positions in the AI ecosystem. However, many AI beneficiaries have yet to be recognized by the market, providing significant latent potential in the portfolio. Examples of this latent potential include software companies with proprietary data, embedded customer relationships, and integrated workflows that can monetize AI through high-margin applications without bearing the full burden of infrastructure investment, positioning these businesses to benefit from the CapEx boom while maintaining strong returns on capital.

Largest Contributors

Alphabet

Alphabet was a top contributor during the quarter, driven by accelerating growth across its core businesses and strong execution in AI-enabled products. Search queries and revenues both accelerated, challenging concerns about competitive threats from AI chat tools and supporting the case for continued innovation-driven market expansion. YouTube and Cloud also posted robust growth, with Cloud backlog and revenues benefiting from strong enterprise demand for AI solutions. Alphabet's disciplined margin management and diversified revenue streams, alongside strategic investments in AI and cloud, position it well for continued growth. Despite ongoing regulatory and competitive pressures, Alphabet's leadership in search, video, and cloud, combined with its ability to monetize a loyal global user base, should support double-digit earnings and revenue growth in the years ahead. We raised the target to an above-average weight position.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing

TSMC was a top contributor during the quarter, driven by robust demand for advanced semiconductor manufacturing and improved gross margins as AI continues to grow strong and the non-AI segment showed signs of recovery. Management raised its revenue growth guidance to the mid-30% range, and given continued strength in demand, AI-related growth targets are expected to move above the current mid-40% level. TSMC's leadership in cutting-edge process nodes remains undisputed. The company raised its CapEx guidance to support future growth and is expected to increase its AI revenue growth target, reflecting confidence in the secular expansion of high-performance computing and AI workloads. Strategic steps to diversify manufacturing outside Taiwan, including new facilities in Japan, Germany, and the U.S., further strengthen TSMC's position. With its technology leadership, strong customer relationships, and disciplined execution, TSMC remains well-positioned to deliver strong double-digit revenue and earnings growth over the coming years. We raised the position target to an above-average weight during the quarter.

Fast Retailing

Fast Retailing was a top contributor during the quarter, driven by strong execution across its global operations. China returned to growth in August and September, with management guiding for further improvement in both revenue and profit in the coming fiscal year, a turnaround following topline decline in China this fiscal year. International operations continued to show significant growth and margin expansion, particularly in North America and Europe, where the company continues to target good growth and further margin improvement. Fast Retailing's scale, efficient supply chain, and ability to offer quality products at attractive prices help navigate macroeconomic headwinds and currency volatility, while ongoing innovation in product design and marketing support brand strength and customer loyalty. With Japan accounting for one-third of Uniqlo's business and international regions poised for further expansion, Fast Retailing remains well positioned to capitalize on global growth opportunities across its diverse markets and deliver double-digit revenue growth in the years ahead. We maintained a below-average weight position during the quarter, trimming on strength.

Largest Detractors

Arm Holdings

Arm Holdings was a detractor during the quarter, despite a strong fiscal Q2 earnings report with revenue up 34% and profit 43%. Royalty revenue grew 21%, driven by triple digits data center growth and higher smartphone royalty from compute subsystem (CSS) customers. License revenue increased 56%, or 17% excluding related-party design service fees from SoftBank (SFTBY). The next quarter guidance for sales growth of 25% and profit up 20% was also ahead of expectations. However, the company faced several headwinds that weighed on investor sentiment. Elevated R&D spending, mostly related to Arm's design service for SoftBank, will impact Arm's margin outlook for next year. In addition, SoftBank is borrowing against its Arm investment to finance its investment into OpenAI, which raised concerns over forced selling of Arm shares if SoftBank does not secure sufficient funding for its investment commitments. Despite these near-term challenges, we remain confident in Arm's growth outlook, and we expect Arm to provide greater clarity over its chip project with SoftBank in 2026. We maintained a below-average weight position during the quarter, adding to the position on weakness.

Microsoft

Microsoft, a global leader in enterprise software and cloud services, was a detractor during the quarter, despite an overall solid quarterly report. Azure growth of 39% came in slightly below expectations, and guidance for the next quarter was weaker than expected, which management asserted was due to supply constraints.

The company's partnership with OpenAI has accelerated investments in infrastructure and the ultimate return on these investments remains difficult to quantify. At the same time, the adoption of Copilot has been lackluster, and Microsoft remains dependent on OpenAI for the core AI features. In our view, the returns on the infrastructure investments should be exciting over time. We also remain confident that Microsoft can develop the LLM expertise in-house or partner with other companies as OpenAI is unlikely to maintain the level of dominance it has had thus far in the AI journey.

Microsoft's business model is anchored by substantial pricing power, high switching costs, and a growing base of recurring revenues as more products transition to cloud and Software-as-a-Service models. While elevated capital expenditures and evolving market dynamics present challenges, Microsoft's strong customer base, leadership in cloud and AI, and disciplined execution position it well for double-digit earnings and revenue growth as the benefits of these investments materialize. We trimmed the position during the quarter, maintaining an above-average weight.

MercadoLibre

MercadoLibre was a detractor during the quarter amid continued macroeconomic and competitive pressures across its e-commerce and fintech segments. Third quarter results were mixed with revenue growth of 39% (49% in constant currency) slightly ahead of expectations driven by Brazil and Mexico while Argentina, which accounts for a substantial portion of profits, was weak due to ongoing macroeconomic pressures. Units sold accelerated to 39% from 31%, helped by lower free shipping thresholds, while shipping costs declined due to more efficient use of excess capacity. However, margins were a bit light, reflecting the impact of free shipping initiatives. In Brazil and Mexico, intensifying competition from global and regional rivals required continued investment in faster delivery, loyalty benefits, and seller support, pressuring unit economics. Credit performance was solid, but net interest margins contracted due to higher funding costs, especially in Argentina. Management remains confident in the company's market leadership, logistics, and broad fintech offerings, and is investing in demand drivers rather than cutting merchant fees like competitors. As the regional leader in both e-commerce and fintech, MercadoLibre benefits from self-reinforcing scale advantages across its interlocking platforms, which should support margin improvement progression despite intensifying competition in the near-term. MercadoLibre's scaled logistics network, expanding advertising monetization, and the region's low e-commerce and fintech penetration position the company well for attractive low-20s earnings and revenue growth trajectories over the next three years. We maintained a below-average weight position.

Portfolio Activity

We initiated new positions in Broadcom, Netflix, and Sea Limited. Two positions were liquidated during the quarter, ServiceNow and Infosys. In the fourth quarter, several positions were added to on weakness including Adyen (ADYEY), Amazon, Arm Holdings, Nvidia, and Salesforce (CRM). Positions in Fast Retailing, HDFC Bank (HDB), and Microsoft among others were trimmed on relative strength to fund compelling opportunities elsewhere.

Purchases

Broadcom

During the quarter, we initiated a below-average weight position in leading semiconductor and infrastructure software company Broadcom. Broadcom's ongoing transformation from a provider of custom ASICs to a full rack-scale solutions vendor, including scale-up networking, marks a significant improvement in its strategic positioning compared to earlier in the year. This strategic evolution has been validated by recent product launches, such as the Scale-up Ethernet solution, and committed orders from major customers like Anthropic. Broadcom is also diversifying its customer base, expanding from three to five qualified customers with purchase commitments, and accelerating production volume timelines for future prospective clients. The shift in AI demand from training to inferencing is creating more visible and stable revenue streams, as inferencing workloads have grown rapidly, driven by innovations in long context windows and agentic workloads.

Broadcom's business model is supported by strong pricing power, recurring revenues, and robust growth opportunities. The company maintains industry-leading margins, with semiconductor gross margin around 70% and software gross margin near 90%, leveraging high technology barriers and strong customer relationships. Recurring revenue is underpinned by long-term AI semiconductor engagements and high-retention infrastructure software subscriptions. As AI spending shifts toward inference, revenue visibility and stability improve, backed by real use cases and multi-year customer commitments. Broadcom's growth is driven by rising AI demand, as major labs and hyperscalers adopt custom ASICs for both training and inference. The company's ability to integrate a full systems solution from ASICs to networking provides an advantage over other vendors, and its strategy of building sustainable franchises ensures resilience against market and technology shifts. While competition and customer concentration remain risks, Broadcom is actively expanding its custom ASIC engagements to improve diversification. Broadcom's strategic evolution and focus on organic growth and AI position it well for long-term stability and profitability, supporting over 20% revenue and earnings growth.

Netflix

During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Netflix, the global leader in streaming entertainment. Netflix leverages its unrivaled scale and content library to exercise consistent pricing power while maintaining industry-low churn rates. The company's recurring subscription model, supported by a highly engaged and loyal user base, generates stable and predictable cash flows. Netflix is well-positioned to capture significant long-term growth as connected TV adoption rises and content offerings expand across geographies. The company's ability to personalize recommendations, invest in original programming, and scale new initiatives such as ad-supported tiers and gaming further enhances its competitive moat and monetization potential. Netflix's shares were impacted by the uncertainty over the pending Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) acquisition. If the company prevails and closes the acquisition, we expect Netflix to leverage Warner's deep IPs library to broaden engagement and global growth and drive meaningful synergies. On the other hand, if the deal falls through, we do not expect meaningful changes to the competitive landscape. Regardless of the outcome of the deal, we believe Netflix is well-positioned to gain further share of global TV viewing, drive margin accretion, and sustain strong double-digit long-term earnings growth as it continues to innovate in content, technology, and distribution. We plan to build the position opportunistically moving forward.

Sea Limited

During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Sea Limited, a Southeast Asian consumer internet company with an integrated ecosystem combining e-commerce, digital payments, and entertainment. Sea has a diversified business model, with its Shopee e-commerce platform, a mobile-centric marketplace that provides integrated payments, logistics infrastructure, and seller services; Garena, a digital entertainment platform for users to access mobile and PC online games; and Monee digital financial services offering credit, mobile wallets, payment processing, banking, and insurtech services. Sea operates in markets with unique geopolitical, cultural, and economic complexities, and the company is ahead in localizing its products and content, making it difficult for competitors to disrupt at scale. Sea's e-commerce business benefits from low penetration rates and rising digital adoption, while its logistics network and localized approach create meaningful competitive advantages that are difficult for competitors to replicate. The company's digital financial services segment is scaling rapidly, leveraging accumulated user data and e-commerce use cases to drive engagement and profitability. In gaming, Sea's deep understanding of local audiences and self-developed franchises like Free Fire are favorable for emerging markets audiences where low-end smartphones dominate. While near-term margin volatility is expected as Sea invests in logistics, fulfillment, and new initiatives such as free shipping membership, management's focus on building long-term competitive advantages and expanding addressable markets positions the company well for sustainable growth.

Among the key risk considerations are increasing competition from TikTok and Lazada (backed by Alibaba (BABA)) in Southeast Asia, MercadoLibre in Brazil, and Coupang (CPNG) in Taiwan. TikTok has been able to rapidly grow market share leveraging its user traffic on social media and the market share dynamic between Shopee, TikTok, and potential new entrants needs to be closely considered. We are also monitoring the unique political, macroeconomic, and currency risks across the multiple markets Sea operates in. Additionally, a potential slowdown in the U.S. and China could weaken the region's economic development and lead to depreciation in local currencies. We believe the company is positioned to benefit from low market penetration and an above average GDP growth rate across its geographies.

Profitability and positive free cash flow are growing strongly supported by the company's earlier investments, which we expect will continue to drive repeatable revenues from a large installed base. We plan to build the position opportunistically moving forward.

Sales

ServiceNow

During the quarter, we liquidated our position in ServiceNow due to forced attrition. While ServiceNow remains a well-positioned leader in enterprise workflow automation, recent developments have increased uncertainty about prospects for the share price. Execution remains strong, but concerns have grown regarding a potential deceleration in 2026 organic growth, a higher valuation multiple, and a shift in strategy from disciplined organic expansion to larger, more aggressive M&A, highlighted by the recently announced acquisition of Armis. This change could introduce greater integration risk and alter the company's historically strong execution profile. Given these factors, we chose to reallocate capital to higher conviction opportunities with clearer visibility into durable growth.

Infosys

We liquidated our position in Infosys during the quarter due to forced attrition. More recently, investor sentiment toward India has cooled, driven by softening domestic demand, persistent tariff uncertainty, and a rotation of foreign capital into markets more exposed to AI and semiconductor growth. Along with other outsourced IT service providers, Infosys' shares have been weak as concerns grew over the potential for these business models to be disrupted by AI, amid a softer demand environment and a change in enterprise spending priorities. While we believe IT services providers remain well positioned to assist with enterprise AI deployment, we had more attractive opportunities in the portfolio to reallocate capital to.

Valuation Opportunity and Conviction Through Extremes

Entering 2026, the portfolio is well positioned. The sustained focus on cyclicality and momentum has driven the quality factor to historically depressed relative levels. The valuation premium for high-quality stocks has compressed to levels typically observed only during periods of crisis. Similarly, the valuation of the SGA Global Growth portfolio relative to the MSCI ACWI is currently at an all-time low. The premium typically placed on reliability and predictability of growth has evaporated in recent years as a result of momentum-chasing amid the AI infrastructure boom.

Relative Valuations Between SGA and ACWI At Most Attractive Levels Since Inception

While the past year has been tough for the portfolio, we are heartened by the resilient growth opportunities across our holdings and the now historically attractive relative valuation of the portfolio. We find it unlikely that the market will continue to ignore the high-quality companies we own and believe the setup moving forward is very attractive as the portfolio is well positioned to benefit from a broadening of market leadership and softening of the extreme momentum dynamics that have dominated markets over the past three years. Our philosophy, process, and approach remain steadfast. We will continue to invest in high-quality companies where we see attractively valued long-term durable growth – an approach that has delivered good returns with lower levels of risk over time.

Thank you for your patience and partnership – we wish you all the best for 2026.

Investment Objective

SGA builds high-conviction portfolios focused on quality growth businesses that are anticipated to achieve consistent mid-teens earnings growth with reduced variability, supported by predictable revenue and cash flow generation. The firm's investment goal is to convert this stable portfolio earnings growth into a return profile designed to protect and reliably compound client wealth over time.
